title: Kafta meatballs
subtitle: Spiced lamb meatballs baked in tomato sauce
excerpt: Lamb meatballs seasoned with cumin, coriander, cinnamon, and fresh herbs, baked in a simple tomato sauce until just cooked through. The spicing is warm rather than hot — complex without being overwhelming. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Kafta (also kofta, keftedes in Greek) is the spiced minced meat preparation found across the Levant, Turkey, and wider Middle East. The baked meatball version with tomato sauce is common in Lebanese and Syrian home cooking, documented by Anissa Helou in Lebanese Cuisine (1994) and Claudia Roden in A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze uncooked or cooked meatballs for up to three months. Defrost overnight in the fridge.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. The meatball mix benefits from chilling before rolling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Roll the meatballs up to 24 hours ahead and refrigerate until ready to bake.

content.text: Kafta is lamb mince at its best: the fat in the mince carries the warm spices through every mouthful without requiring much preparation. The key is to use lamb mince with a good fat content — very lean lamb makes dry meatballs. A 20% fat content is ideal. Do not overwork the mix, and chill the rolled meatballs for 30 minutes before baking so they hold their shape in the oven.

items.prepNote: grated on the coarse side of a box grater, moisture squeezed out

content.text: Combine {{lamb-mince}} of lamb mince, the grated onion (squeezed of moisture), {{garlic}} of grated garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, {{parsley-flat}} of parsley, {{mint}} of mint, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Mix briefly until just combined. Roll into balls about 4 cm across — you should get 16–20 meatballs. Place on a tray, cover, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Warm {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a small saucepan over medium heat. Cook the second onion for 6 minutes until soft. Add the second measure of garlic and {{cumin-ground}} of cumin; stir for 1 minute. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Simmer for 8 minutes. Pour the sauce into a roasting tin. Nestle the meatballs into the sauce in a single layer. Bake for 20–25 minutes until the meatballs are cooked through and browning on top. Serve with rice, flatbread, or bulgur wheat.

items.symptom: Meatballs falling apart in the sauce
items.cause: Onion not squeezed dry, making the mix too wet to bind. Or mix overworked and fat broken down.
items.fix: Squeeze the grated onion in a clean cloth before adding to the mince. Mix only until combined, not longer.
Use lamb mince with at least 15% fat. Very lean mince makes kafta that tastes more like a health product than a meal.
Ground spices lose potency quickly. If the cumin and coriander smell faint in the jar, use 50% more than the recipe states.

content.text: Kafta (from the Persian word for pounded) is the spiced minced meat mixture that runs through the cooking of the Middle East, Central Asia, and the Indian subcontinent under various names: kofta in India and Pakistan, köfte in Turkey, keftedes in Greece. The Levantine version is lamb-based, heavily herbed, and served with tomato sauce and flatbread. It appears at Lebanese and Syrian family tables as a weekday dinner and at restaurants as part of a mezze spread. It is the kind of dish that tastes more complicated than it is to make.
